item.title: More Canadians willing to serve in Armed Forces during major conflict, poll suggests
item.description: Fifty-eight per cent of respondents view the Armed Forces positively, and about three-quarters say they make them proud to be Canadian

item.title: AI being used to add fake details in immigration, asylum applications, federal officials say
item.description: The applications, which include references to fabricated court decisions, are creating a fresh challenge, Immigration and Refugee Board says

item.title: Former MP behind sports gambling legalization says some results are 'deplorable'
item.description: The former MP who spent more than a decade pushing to legalize single-event sports betting says he doesn't regret it — but says its implementation is in some ways "sad" and "deplorable."

item.title: Cold front: Inside NATO's race to secure the Arctic
item.description: Neglected for years, the Arctic is now NATO’s critical front, driven by urgent threats from Russian militarization and shifting geopolitics. While launching "Arctic Sentry" to bridge surveillance gaps, the alliance is struggling with extreme conditions, vast distances and the need for new, rugged technology to maintain a persistent presence in the inhospitable Far North.

item.title: These three byelections could give Mark Carney a majority government
item.description: Three upcoming byelections could determine not just three local contests but whether Prime Minister Mark Carney gets a majority mandate to gov…

item.title: One Yonge is Toronto’s most famous address. Why residents say developers’ plaza plans for it are underwhelming
item.description: “Just putting a couple of tree sticks in the ground and a couple of benches isn’t going to cut it.”

item.title: Toronto woman to walk 300 km, visit all 100 Toronto libraries in memory of mother
Language classes cut, reading program axed and bell times changed: The ‘massive’ overhaul shaking TCDSB families
Trump's respect for King Charles possibly quashed desire to annex Canada, says royal commentator
item.description: An upcoming book authored by a prominent royal commentator says U.S. President Donald Trump was primarily interested in annexing Canadian territory just above the U.S. border — and his respect for King Charles may have quashed his interest.

item.title: Protest outside Brampton Hindu temple targets alleged Indian state interference
Spy allegations against veteran Ottawa journalist drew attention from RCMP security unit
Toronto has given AI the green light to tackle its traffic woes. Here’s where the ‘smart’ intersections are coming first
item.description: Toronto hopes artificial intelligence will make traffic run more smoothly along some of the city's busiest streets.

item.title: Doug Ford promised HART Hubs would help the drug crisis. A year later, here’s what they’ve accomplished in Toronto
item.description: Wait times for residential treatment are long, demand for housing outstrips supply and some clients stopped coming after consumption sites closed.

item.title: Eglinton LRT hours extended past midnight
Could Trump follow through on his threats to quit NATO? Experts say it’s possible
Stephen Lewis, longtime Ontario NDP politician and diplomat, dies at 88
Doug Ford’s government won’t hand over bike lane emails. Why critics say it’s a sign of things to come
The Doug Ford government is moving to shield politicians from scrutiny. Here are secrets the new law would have kept under wraps
Ban on rescue dogs from countries at risk of spreading rabies to remain, CFIA says
item.description: The federal government says a ban on the import of rescue dogs into Canada will remain in place until further notice due to rabies concerns. Animal advocacy groups say the restrictions — which were brought in more than three years ago and originally meant to be temporary — are preventing healthy animals from legitimate international rescues from finding homes in Canada.

item.title: These Ontario immigration programs are being hit hard by federal budget cuts. Here’s what they are losing
Burdett 'Burd' Sisler, war veteran and oldest known living Canadian, has died at 110
item.description: Burdett "Burd" Sisler, the oldest known living Canadian — and one of the world's oldest Second World War veterans — has died at 110, his family confirmed on Saturday.

item.title: Governor-General’s husband says the couple are planning their exit from Rideau Hall
item.description: Former journalist Whit Fraser, who spoke with The Globe ahead of the release of his memoir, says they’re looking for an apartment

item.title: How the war in the Middle East is already impacting mortgage rates in Canada
item.description: The ongoing war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are impacting Canadian pocketbooks in ways we might not expect. Experts warn Canadian homeowners facing mortgage renewals that rates are going up, despite the Bank of Canada holding its key interest rate for months.

item.title: Alberta’s proposed immigration bill would create more red tape: hospitality sector
item.description: The hospitality sector says the bill requiring employers to be registered and be licensed to hire foreign workers creates more barriers when the industry is already struggling.

item.title: Doug Ford eyes a byelection win in Scarborough amid Liberal and NDP troubles
B.C. First Nations criticize suspension of DRIPA as ‘absolute betrayal’
item.description: Leaked transcript of meeting between Indigenous leaders, Premier Eby shows them accusing him of colonialism

item.title: Champagne leaves China with pork tariffs still in place, but touts relationship-building
item.description: Although Chinese tariffs on Canadian pork products remain in place after a visit to China by Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne, he says the important task is relationship-building — and it's not realistic to expect immediate solutions.

item.title: Advanced voting begins in 3 key byelections. What to know
item.description: The byelections this month are in two Liberal stronghold seats in the Toronto area and one contested riding in Quebec, north of Montreal.

item.title: Canada, China sign pledge in Beijing to deepen financial-sector ties
item.description: Canada’s Finance Minister says he raised the importance of labour standards during meetings with top Chinese officials

item.title: Ottawa unveils $51-million in additional aid for Ukraine
item.description: More than half will go toward humanitarian aid through outside organizations, and $5-million will go toward programming for veterans

item.title: Quebec law banning public prayer looms over Way of the Cross marches in province
item.description: Head of Assembly of Quebec Catholic Bishops says new secularism law turns religious people into ‘second-class citizens’

item.title: Young Canadians are increasingly miserable. Government priorities show why
item.description: Governments have not matched the rise in spending on boomers with new revenue from that generation
